title: Dark Spots and Pigmentation: Evening Out Your Skin
excerpt: Ayurveda calls dark spots and uneven skin tone "Vyanga," a condition rooted in imbalanced doshas, poor digestion, and sun exposure, and classical granthas describe a whole-body approach to clearing the complexion from the inside out.
body_html:
that stubborn patch you keep trying to cover

Most of us have been there: a dark spot appears on the cheek or forehead, we try a cream, it fades a little, and then the next summer it comes back darker. Pigmentation, melasma, post-acne marks, the tan that never quite leaves, all of these feel like cosmetic problems. Ayurveda sees them differently. It treats the skin as a mirror of what is happening deeper in the body, particularly in the blood and the digestive fire.

The classical name for this condition is Vyanga (pronounced vyaan-ga), which refers to dark, painless discoloration on the face caused by a vitiation of Vata and Pitta doshas. Sushruta Samhita classifies Vyanga under minor skin disorders (kshudra kushtha) and describes it as thin, discolored patches that arise when Vata carries aggravated Pitta into the rakta dhatu, the blood tissue (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana). In simpler terms: excess heat and movement in the body deposit pigment unevenly in the skin.

the digestion and blood connection

Ayurveda's most important insight about skin pigmentation is that it does not begin at the skin. It begins in the gut. When digestion (agni) is sluggish or irregular, the body produces ama, a term for incompletely processed metabolic waste. This ama circulates with the blood and, when Pitta is simultaneously elevated, creates the environment for uneven pigment deposition. Bhavaprakasha notes that conditions affecting the complexion are closely tied to the quality of rakta dhatu and the health of ranjaka Pitta, the sub-dosha responsible for the colour and quality of blood (Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda).

Pitta-aggravating factors are the most common triggers in everyday life: long hours in the afternoon sun, irregular mealtimes, excess sour or fermented food, unresolved stress, and disrupted sleep. For many women, hormonal changes during pregnancy or around the menstrual cycle can also spike Pitta and intensify pigmentation. Yoga Ratnakara connects Vyanga specifically to Pitta-Vata imbalance and lists causative factors that include heat exposure, anger, and poor dietary habits (Yoga Ratnakara, Kshudra Roga Prakarana).

everyday habits that gently correct the imbalance

The Ayurvedic approach to Vyanga works on two fronts at once: cooling and clearing Pitta from the inside, while gently nourishing the skin from the outside. Neither alone is usually enough. Bhavaprakasha's Nighantu describes several herbs known for their rakta-prasadana, or blood-purifying and complexion-brightening, properties. Manjistha, lodhra, and haridra (turmeric) are among the most consistently cited. Turmeric in particular, used internally in warm milk or as part of the daily diet, has been valued across multiple granthas for supporting clear skin (Bhavaprakasha Nighantu, Haritakyadi Varga).

On the food side, reducing Pitta means favouring cooling, lightly spiced, freshly cooked meals. Favour coriander, fennel, and cumin for seasoning. Reduce very sour foods such as excess tamarind, vinegar, and fermented pickles. Drink warm or room-temperature water through the day. Eating at consistent times matters more than people expect: erratic mealtimes directly disturb agni and, over time, affect rakta dhatu quality.

For the skin itself, the classical approach includes gentle exfoliation using ubtan, a paste traditionally made with chickpea flour, turmeric, and sandalwood, applied before bathing. Sushruta Samhita recommends regular self-massage with suitable oils before bathing (abhyanga) as part of the dinacharya, or daily routine, for maintaining skin health (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana). Sun protection is also clearly implied in classical caution about midday sun exposure as a Pitta-aggravating factor. Covering the face or using a gentle physical barrier during peak sun hours is entirely consistent with classical guidance, even if sunscreen did not exist as a product.

when to see a doctor

The habits described here are general wellness practices grounded in classical Ayurvedic texts. They are not a diagnosis, and they are not a substitute for medical care. If your pigmentation is new, rapidly changing, asymmetric, raised, itchy, or bleeding, please see a dermatologist promptly, as these can be signs of conditions that need clinical evaluation. If you are pregnant, dark patches on the face (melasma) are common but should be discussed with your obstetrician before starting any herbal regimen. A qualified Ayurvedic vaidya can assess your prakriti and current dosha state and recommend herbs and treatments that are appropriate for your specific situation, rather than a generic protocol.

Try This Today

  • Golden milk at night: Stir a quarter teaspoon of turmeric and a pinch of black pepper into a cup of warm milk before bed. Both ingredients are cited in Bhavaprakasha for supporting clear skin and blood quality.
  • Fennel-coriander digestive water: Boil one teaspoon each of fennel and coriander seeds in two cups of water for five minutes, strain, and sip warm through the morning. This gently cools Pitta and supports agni without over-stimulating it.
  • Simple ubtan paste: Mix two tablespoons of chickpea flour with a pinch of turmeric and enough water or rose water to make a paste. Apply to the face, let it dry for ten minutes, then rinse with cool water. Use two or three times a week.
  • Eat at consistent times: Ayurveda regards erratic mealtimes as a direct cause of agni disturbance. Aim to eat your main meal at the same time each day, preferably around midday when digestive fire is strongest.
  • Avoid afternoon sun on the face: Classical texts flag midday and afternoon sun as a primary Pitta-aggravating factor. Covering the face or staying in shade between 11 am and 3 pm is one of the simplest and most effective protective habits.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Coriander (धनिया)

A cooling Pitta-pacifying spice used in digestive waters and daily cooking to reduce internal heat linked to pigmentation.

Fennel (सौंफ)

Gently cools and aids digestion without over-stimulating agni; commonly paired with coriander in Pitta-balancing teas.

Cumin (जीरा)

Supports digestive fire while being mild enough for Pitta types; helps clear ama that can cloud the complexion.

metafields.dek: Ayurveda calls dark spots and uneven skin tone "Vyanga," a condition rooted in imbalanced doshas, poor digestion, and sun exposure, and classical granthas describe a whole-body approach to clearing the complexion from the inside out.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Dark spots (Vyanga) arise from Pitta-Vata imbalance affecting the blood, not just the skin surface.
Poor digestion creates ama that circulates in the blood and causes uneven pigment deposition.
Cooling foods, consistent mealtimes, turmeric, and gentle sun protection address the root cause.

glossary_terms.definition: Dark, painless discoloration on the face; the classical Ayurvedic term for pigmentation and melasma.
The blood tissue, one of the seven dhatus; its quality directly influences skin colour and clarity.
The sub-dosha of Pitta responsible for the colour and vitality of blood; imbalance here is linked to uneven complexion.
Incompletely processed metabolic waste produced when digestion is weak; circulates in the body and contributes to tissue imbalance.
The quality of purifying or clarifying the blood; herbs with this action are used to improve complexion from within.
A traditional paste applied to the skin before bathing, typically made with chickpea flour, turmeric, and other skin-supporting ingredients.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana (classification of skin disorders including Vyanga)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana (daily regimen including abhyanga and sun caution)
Bhavaprakasha, Madhyama Khanda (rakta dhatu, ranjaka Pitta, and complexion)
Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu (haridra, manjistha, and lodhra among herbs supporting blood quality and complexion)
Yoga Ratnakara, Kshudra Roga Prakarana (Vyanga, causative factors, Pitta-Vata imbalance)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Vyanga" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Vyanga is defined as: Dark, painless discoloration on the face; the classical Ayurvedic term for pigmentation and melasma.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to golden milk at night. Stir a quarter teaspoon of turmeric and a pinch of black pepper into a cup of warm milk before bed. Both ingredients are cited in Bhavaprakasha for supporting clear skin and blood quality.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Coriander for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Coriander is highly regarded. A cooling Pitta-pacifying spice used in digestive waters and daily cooking to reduce internal heat linked to pigmentation.
